# DOM Traversal in JavaScript

**DOM Traversal** means navigating through HTML elements in the DOM tree.

&lt;div id="parent"&gt;&nbsp; &lt;h2&gt;Title&lt;/h2&gt;&nbsp; &lt;ul&gt;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; &lt;li&gt;React&lt;/li&gt;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; &lt;li&gt;Angular&lt;/li&gt;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; &lt;li&gt;Vue&lt;/li&gt;&nbsp; &lt;/ul&gt;&lt;/div&gt;![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

DOM Structure:

```text
div
 ├── h2
 └── ul
      ├── li
      ├── li
      └── li
```

***

# Parent Traversal

## parentElement

```javascript
const li =
  document.querySelector("li");

console.log(
  li.parentElement
);
```

Output:

&lt;ul&gt;![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

***

## parentNode

```javascript
console.log(
  li.parentNode
);
```

Returns parent node.

***

# Child Traversal

## children

```javascript
const parent =
  document.getElementById(
    "parent"
  );

console.log(
  parent.children
);
```

Output:

```javascript
HTMLCollection(2)
```

Contains:

&lt;h2&gt;&lt;ul&gt;![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

***

## firstElementChild

```javascript
console.log(
  parent.firstElementChild
);
```

Output:

&lt;h2&gt;Title&lt;/h2&gt;![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

***

## lastElementChild

```javascript
console.log(
  parent.lastElementChild
);
```

Output:

&lt;ul&gt;![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

***

# Sibling Traversal

## nextElementSibling

```javascript
const heading =
  document.querySelector("h2");

console.log(
  heading.nextElementSibling
);
```

Output:

&lt;ul&gt;![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

***

## previousElementSibling

```javascript
const ul =
  document.querySelector("ul");

console.log(
  ul.previousElementSibling
);
```

Output:

&lt;h2&gt;![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

***

# Traversing All Children

```javascript
const list =
  document.querySelector("ul");

for (let child of list.children) {

  console.log(
    child.textContent
  );
}
```

Output:

```text
React
Angular
Vue
```

***

# Closest Method

Very useful in event delegation.

&lt;button class="save"&gt;&nbsp; Save&lt;/button&gt;![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

```javascript
const button =
  document.querySelector(
    ".save"
  );

console.log(
  button.closest("div")
);
```

Returns nearest matching ancestor.

***

# React Example Using useRef

DOM traversal is rarely needed in React, but sometimes useful.

```jsx
import {
  useRef
} from "react";

function App() {

  const inputRef =
    useRef();

  const handleClick =
    () => {

      console.log(
        inputRef.current
          .parentElement
      );
    };

  return (
    <div>

      <input
        ref={inputRef}
      />

      <button
        onClick={
          handleClick
        }
      >
        Check Parent
      </button>

    </div>
  );
}
```

***

# Traversing Table Rows

&lt;table&gt;&nbsp; &lt;tr&gt;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; &lt;td&gt;Sudhir&lt;/td&gt;&nbsp; &lt;/tr&gt;&lt;/table&gt;![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

```javascript
const td =
  document.querySelector(
    "td"
  );

console.log(
  td.parentElement
);
```

Output:

&lt;tr&gt;![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

***

# Event Delegation + Traversal

Interview favourite.

&lt;ul id="list"&gt;&nbsp; &lt;li&gt;React&lt;/li&gt;&nbsp; &lt;li&gt;Angular&lt;/li&gt;&nbsp; &lt;li&gt;Vue&lt;/li&gt;&lt;/ul&gt;![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

```javascript
document
  .getElementById("list")
  .addEventListener(
    "click",
    event => {

      console.log(
        event.target
          .textContent
      );

      console.log(
        event.target
          .parentElement
      );
    }
  );
```

Benefits:

```text
Single Event Listener
Less Memory Usage
Dynamic Elements Supported
```

***

# Common DOM Traversal Methods

| Method                 | Description               |
| ---------------------- | ------------------------- |
| parentElement          | Parent HTML element       |
| parentNode             | Parent node               |
| children               | All child elements        |
| childNodes             | All child nodes           |
| firstElementChild      | First child element       |
| lastElementChild       | Last child element        |
| nextElementSibling     | Next sibling              |
| previousElementSibling | Previous sibling          |
| closest()              | Nearest matching ancestor |

***

# Interview Questions

### Q1. What is DOM Traversal?

Navigating between parent, child, and sibling elements in the DOM tree.

***

### Q2. Difference Between children and childNodes?

```javascript
element.children
```

Returns only HTML elements.

```javascript
element.childNodes
```

Returns:

```text
Elements
Text Nodes
Comments
```

***

### Q3. What is closest()?

Returns the nearest ancestor matching a selector.

```javascript
element.closest(".card")
```

***

### Q4. How is DOM Traversal used in React?

Typically with:

```javascript
useRef()
```

Examples:

```text
Focus Management
Scroll Handling
Measurements
Event Delegation
Third-Party Libraries
```

***

# React Interview Example

```jsx
const cardRef = useRef();

useEffect(() => {

  console.log(
    cardRef.current
      .parentElement
  );

}, []);
```

Used when integrating with:

```text
Charts
Maps
Legacy jQuery
Canvas
Drag & Drop
```

***

# Senior React Interview Answer

> DOM Traversal is the process of navigating between parent, child, and sibling elements in the DOM tree. Common methods include `parentElement`, `children`, `firstElementChild`, `lastElementChild`, `nextElementSibling`, `previousElementSibling`, and `closest()`. In React, direct DOM traversal is uncommon because React encourages a declarative approach, but it is sometimes used with `useRef` for focus management, measurements, scrolling, drag-and-drop, and third-party library integration.


# DOM Traversal with Event Delegation

**Event Delegation** means attaching **one event listener to a parent element** instead of multiple listeners to child elements.

This improves:

```text
✅ Performance
✅ Memory Usage
✅ Dynamic Element Handling
```

***

## HTML

&lt;ul id="users"&gt;&nbsp; &lt;li data-id="1"&gt;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; Sudhir&nbsp; &lt;/li&gt;&nbsp; &lt;li data-id="2"&gt;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; John&nbsp; &lt;/li&gt;&nbsp; &lt;li data-id="3"&gt;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; Smith&nbsp; &lt;/li&gt;&lt;/ul&gt;`![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

***

## JavaScript

```javascript
const userList =
  document.getElementById(
    "users"
  );

userList.addEventListener(
  "click",
  event => {

    // Clicked Element
    const target =
      event.target;

    // DOM traversal
    const parent =
      target.parentElement;

    console.log(
      "Clicked User:",
      target.textContent
    );

    console.log(
      "User Id:",
      target.dataset.id
    );

    console.log(
      "Parent:",
      parent.id
    );
  }
);
```

***

## Traversal Flow

```text
<li>
  ↓ parentElement
<ul>
  ↓ parentElement
<body>
```

***

# React Example Using useRef for DOM Traversal

React generally prefers a declarative approach, but sometimes you need to access DOM elements directly.

***

## Example: Find Parent Element

```jsx
import {
  useRef
} from "react";

function App() {

  const inputRef =
    useRef(null);

  const handleClick =
    () => {

      const parent =
        inputRef.current
          .parentElement;

      console.log(parent);

      console.log(
        parent.tagName
      );
    };

  return (
    <div
      className="container"
    >

      <input
        ref={inputRef}
        placeholder="type here"
      />

      <button
        onClick={
          handleClick
        }
      >
        Find Parent
      </button>

    </div>
  );
}

export default App;
```

***

# Traversing Children

```jsx
function App() {

  const listRef =
    useRef(null);

  const showChildren =
    () => {

      const children =
        listRef.current
          .children;

      [...children].forEach(
        child => {

          console.log(
            child.textContent
          );

        }
      );
    };

  return (
    <>
      <ul ref={listRef}>
        <li>React</li>
        <li>Angular</li>
        <li>Vue</li>
      </ul>

      <button
        onClick={
          showChildren
        }
      >
        Show Children
      </button>
    </>
  );
}
```

***

# Using closest()

Very useful in React tables and lists.

```jsx
const handleClick = (
  event
) => {

  const row =
    event.target.closest(
      ".table-row"
    );

  console.log(row);
};
```

***

## Example

&lt;tr class="table-row"&gt;&nbsp; &lt;td&gt;Edit&lt;/td&gt;&lt;/tr&gt;![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

Clicking inside the row:

```javascript
event.target.closest(
  ".table-row"
);
```

returns:

&lt;tr&gt;![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

***

# Common DOM Traversal Pitfalls in React

## ❌ Using querySelector Directly

Bad:

```javascript
document.querySelector(
  ".input"
);
```

Why?

```text
Bypasses React
Harder to Test
Can Break Re-renders
```

Prefer:

```jsx
const inputRef =
  useRef();
```

***

## ❌ Mutating DOM Manually

Bad:

```javascript
element.innerHTML =
  "Updated";
```

React may overwrite it during the next render.

Prefer:

```jsx
setState(...)
```

***

## ❌ Accessing Ref Before Mount

Bad:

```javascript
console.log(
  inputRef.current
);
```

during initial render.

Output:

```javascript
null
```

Use:

```jsx
useEffect(() => {
  console.log(
    inputRef.current
  );
}, []);
```

***

## ❌ Too Much Parent Traversal

Bad:

```javascript
element.parentElement
  .parentElement
  .parentElement;
```

Hard to maintain.

Use:

```javascript
closest(".card");
```

instead.

***

## ❌ Mixing React State with Direct DOM Updates

Bad:

```jsx
inputRef.current.value =
  "Sudhir";
```

and

```jsx
setValue("Sudhir");
```

simultaneously.

Results:

```text
Two Sources of Truth
```

Prefer controlled components.

***

## ❌ Ref for Everything

Bad:

```jsx
useRef()
```

for state management.

Use:

```jsx
useState()
```

for UI state.

Use:

```jsx
useRef()
```

only for DOM access.

***

# Real React Use Cases for DOM Traversal

### Focus Management

```jsx
inputRef.current.focus();
```

***

### Scroll to Element

```jsx
sectionRef.current.scrollIntoView();
```

***

### Measuring Elements

```jsx
const width =
  divRef.current.offsetWidth;
```

***

### Drag & Drop

```jsx
itemRef.current.parentElement;
```

***

### Event Delegation in Lists

```jsx
event.target.closest(
  ".list-item"
);
```

***

# Interview Answer

**When do you use DOM traversal in React?**

> DOM traversal should be used sparingly in React and typically through `useRef`. Common scenarios include focus management, scrolling, measuring element size, drag-and-drop interactions, and integrating third-party libraries. For traversing the DOM, methods such as `parentElement`, `children`, `nextElementSibling`, and `closest()` are useful. Avoid direct DOM manipulation (`querySelector`, `innerHTML`) when React state can achieve the same result more safely and predictably.



# 1. React Example Using `closest()` for Event Delegation

Event delegation allows one click handler to manage all child elements.

### Example: User List

```jsx
function UserList() {
  const users = [
    { id: 1, name: "Sudhir" },
    { id: 2, name: "John" },
    { id: 3, name: "David" }
  ];

  const handleClick = (event) => {
    const userCard =
      event.target.closest(".user-card");

    if (!userCard) return;

    console.log(
      "User ID:",
      userCard.dataset.id
    );
  };

  return (
    <div onClick={handleClick}>
      {users.map(user => (
        <div
          key={user.id}
          className="user-card"
          data-id={user.id}
        >
          <h3>{user.name}</h3>
          <button>Edit</button>
        </div>
      ))}
    </div>
  );
}
```

***

### DOM Traversal Flow

```text
Button Click
    ↓
event.target
    ↓
closest(".user-card")
    ↓
Parent Card Found
```

Even if the user clicks:

&lt;button&gt;Edit&lt;/button&gt;![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

`closest()` finds:

&lt;div class="user-card"&gt;![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

***

# 2. Managing Focus with useRef

A very common React interview topic.

***

## Auto Focus Input

```jsx
import {
  useEffect,
  useRef
} from "react";

function LoginForm() {

  const emailRef =
    useRef(null);

  useEffect(() => {
    emailRef.current.focus();
  }, []);

  return (
    <input
      ref={emailRef}
      placeholder="Email"
    />
  );
}
```

### Result

```text
Page Loads
    ↓
Input Automatically Focused
```

***

## Focus Next Input

```jsx
import {
  useRef
} from "react";

function Form() {

  const passwordRef =
    useRef(null);

  const handleEnter =
    (event) => {

      if (
        event.key === "Enter"
      ) {

        passwordRef.current
          .focus();
      }
    };

  return (
    <>
      <input
        placeholder="Username"
        onKeyDown={
          handleEnter
        }
      />

      <input
        ref={passwordRef}
        placeholder="Password"
      />
    </>
  );
}
```

***

## Focus Invalid Field

```jsx
const emailRef =
  useRef();

function submit() {

  if (!email) {

    emailRef.current
      .focus();

    return;
  }
}
```

Very useful for:

```text
Forms
Validation
Accessibility (WCAG)
```

***

# 3. Best Practices to Avoid DOM Traversal Issues in React

### ✅ Prefer State Over DOM Manipulation

❌ Bad

```javascript
element.innerHTML =
  "Hello";
```

✅ Good

```jsx
setMessage("Hello");
```

React updates the DOM.

***

### ✅ Prefer useRef Over querySelector

❌ Bad

```javascript
document.querySelector(
  ".input"
);
```

✅ Good

```jsx
const inputRef =
  useRef();
```

***

### ✅ Use closest() Instead of Multiple parentElement Calls

❌ Bad

```javascript
event.target
  .parentElement
  .parentElement
  .parentElement;
```

✅ Good

```javascript
event.target.closest(
  ".card"
);
```

***

### ✅ Access Refs After Mount

❌ Bad

```jsx
console.log(
  inputRef.current
);
```

During render:

```javascript
null
```

✅ Good

```jsx
useEffect(() => {
  console.log(
    inputRef.current
  );
}, []);
```

***

### ✅ Avoid Mixing DOM Updates with React State

❌ Bad

```jsx
inputRef.current.value =
  "Sudhir";

setName("Sudhir");
```

Two sources of truth.

✅ Good

```jsx
setName("Sudhir");
```

Controlled component.

***

### ✅ Use Event Delegation for Large Lists

Instead of:

```jsx
1000 buttons
1000 listeners
```

Use:

```jsx
1 parent listener
```

Benefits:

```text
Better Performance
Less Memory
Cleaner Code
```

***

### ✅ Use Refs Only For DOM Access

Good use cases:

```text
Focus Management
Scrolling
Measuring Elements
Canvas
Charts
Drag & Drop
Third-party Libraries
```

Avoid using refs for:

```text
Business Logic
Application State
API Data
```

Use:

```jsx
useState
useReducer
Context
Redux
```

instead.

***

# Senior React Interview Answer

> In React, DOM traversal should be minimised and performed through `useRef` when necessary. Event delegation combined with `closest()` is an efficient way to handle interactions in large lists because a single parent listener can manage all child events. `useRef` is commonly used for focus management, scrolling, element measurement, drag-and-drop behaviour, and third-party integrations. Best practices include preferring React state over direct DOM manipulation, avoiding `querySelector`, using `closest()` for ancestor lookups, and ensuring refs are accessed only after components have mounted.
Yes. A **File Explorer** is one of the best real-world examples of **DOM navigation + keyboard accessibility + focus management** in React.

Think about Windows Explorer:

```text
Folder
 ├── Documents
 ├── Images
 └── Videos
```

Users should be able to:

✅ Navigate using Arrow Keys

✅ Focus folders/files

✅ Expand/collapse folders

✅ Tab correctly

✅ Screen readers announce selection

✅ Avoid mouse dependency (WCAG)

***

# Accessible File Explorer Example

## Data

```jsx
const files = [
  {
    id: 1,
    name: "Documents"
  },
  {
    id: 2,
    name: "Images"
  },
  {
    id: 3,
    name: "Videos"
  }
];
```

***

# Component

```jsx
import {
  useRef,
  useState
} from "react";

function FileExplorer() {

  const [selectedIndex,
         setSelectedIndex] =
    useState(0);

  const itemRefs =
    useRef([]);

  const handleKeyDown =
    (event) => {

      if (
        event.key === "ArrowDown"
      ) {

        const nextIndex =
          Math.min(
            selectedIndex + 1,
            files.length - 1
          );

        setSelectedIndex(
          nextIndex
        );

        itemRefs.current[
          nextIndex
        ]?.focus();
      }

      if (
        event.key === "ArrowUp"
      ) {

        const previousIndex =
          Math.max(
            selectedIndex - 1,
            0
          );

        setSelectedIndex(
          previousIndex
        );

        itemRefs.current[
          previousIndex
        ]?.focus();
      }
    };

  return (

    <div
      role="tree"
      aria-label="File Explorer"
      onKeyDown={
        handleKeyDown
      }
    >

      {files.map(
        (file, index) => (

          <div
            key={file.id}

            ref={element =>
              (
                itemRefs.current[
                  index
                ] = element
              )
            }

            role="treeitem"

            tabIndex={
              selectedIndex ===
              index
                ? 0
                : -1
            }

            aria-selected={
              selectedIndex ===
              index
            }

            className={
              selectedIndex ===
              index
                ? "active"
                : ""
            }
          >
            {file.name}
          </div>

        )
      )}

    </div>
  );
}

export default FileExplorer;
```

***

# Focus Flow

```text
Documents
     ↓ Arrow Down
Images
     ↓ Arrow Down
Videos
```

Focus moves using:

```jsx
itemRefs.current[index]
  .focus();
```

***

# DOM Navigation

Current Focused Item

```jsx
const active =
  document.activeElement;
```

Parent Navigation

```jsx
active.parentElement;
```

Sibling Navigation

```jsx
active.nextElementSibling;

active.previousElementSibling;
```

However in React:

✅ Prefer refs

❌ Avoid excessive DOM traversal

***

# Tree Structure Example

```text
Root
 ├── Documents
 │     ├── Resume.pdf
 │     └── Notes.docx
 │
 └── Images
       ├── Cat.jpg
       └── Dog.jpg
```

Accessibility Roles:

```jsx
role="tree"

role="treeitem"

aria-expanded

aria-selected
```

Example

```jsx
<div
  role="treeitem"
  aria-expanded="true"
>
  Documents
</div>
```

Screen Reader:

```text
Documents Expanded
```

***

# Using closest() for Event Delegation

Instead of attaching handlers to every file.

```jsx
function handleClick(
  event
) {

  const fileNode =
    event.target.closest(
      "[role='treeitem']"
    );

  if (!fileNode) return;

  console.log(
    fileNode.textContent
  );
}
```

Usage

```jsx
<div
  role="tree"
  onClick={handleClick}
>
```

One listener handles all files.

***

# Accessibility Best Practices

### Roving Tabindex

Only one item should be tabbable.

```jsx
tabIndex={
  selectedIndex === index
    ? 0
    : -1
}
```

***

### Keyboard Navigation

Support:

```text
Arrow Up
Arrow Down
Arrow Left
Arrow Right
Home
End
Enter
Space
```

***

### Announce Selection

```jsx
aria-selected="true"
```

Screen reader says:

```text
Documents Selected
```

***

### Expanded State

```jsx
aria-expanded="true"
```

Screen reader says:

```text
Documents Expanded
```

***

### Focus Visible

```css
.treeitem:focus {
  outline: 2px solid blue;
}
```

Keyboard users can see focus.

***

# Senior React Interview Answer

A file explorer is a classic example of **DOM navigation with accessibility**. In React, focus should be managed using `useRef` rather than direct DOM traversal. An accessible file tree uses:

```text
role="tree"
role="treeitem"
aria-expanded
aria-selected
```

combined with keyboard navigation:

```text
Arrow Keys
Enter
Space
```

A common implementation stores item references in an array of refs and uses a **roving tabindex** pattern so only the active item is focusable. Event delegation with `closest()` can be used to handle clicks efficiently while keeping the component WCAG-compliant and keyboard accessible.


# Accessible File Explorer (React) – Complete Example

This example demonstrates:

✅ Nested folders

✅ Keyboard navigation

✅ Focus management with `useRef`

✅ Arrow Left / Right folder expand-collapse

✅ Arrow Up / Down navigation

✅ `role="tree"` and `role="treeitem"`

✅ WCAG-friendly implementation

***

# What ARIA Roles Mean

### Tree Container

```jsx
role="tree"
```

Tells screen readers:

```text
This is a hierarchical tree view
```

***

### Tree Item

```jsx
role="treeitem"
```

Represents:

```text
Folder
File
Node
```

***

### Expanded State

```jsx
aria-expanded={true}
```

Screen reader:

```text
Documents expanded
```

***

### Selected State

```jsx
aria-selected={true}
```

Screen reader:

```text
Documents selected
```

***

# File Data

```jsx
const treeData = [
  {
    id: "documents",
    name: "Documents",
    children: [
      {
        id: "resume",
        name: "Resume.pdf"
      },
      {
        id: "notes",
        name: "Notes.docx"
      }
    ]
  },
  {
    id: "images",
    name: "Images",
    children: [
      {
        id: "cat",
        name: "Cat.jpg"
      },
      {
        id: "dog",
        name: "Dog.jpg"
      }
    ]
  }
];
```

***

# Complete React Component

```jsx
import React,
{
  useState,
  useRef,
  useEffect
} from "react";

const treeData = [
  {
    id: "documents",
    name: "Documents",
    children: [
      {
        id: "resume",
        name: "Resume.pdf"
      },
      {
        id: "notes",
        name: "Notes.docx"
      }
    ]
  },
  {
    id: "images",
    name: "Images",
    children: [
      {
        id: "cat",
        name: "Cat.jpg"
      },
      {
        id: "dog",
        name: "Dog.jpg"
      }
    ]
  }
];

export default function FileExplorer() {

  const [expanded,
         setExpanded] =
    useState({
      documents: true,
      images: false
    });

  const [focusedIndex,
         setFocusedIndex] =
    useState(0);

  const nodeRefs =
    useRef([]);

  const flattenTree =
    () => {

      const result = [];

      treeData.forEach(folder => {

        result.push({
          ...folder,
          isFolder: true
        });

        if (
          expanded[folder.id]
        ) {

          folder.children?.forEach(
            file => {

              result.push({
                ...file,
                parent:
                  folder.id,
                isFolder: false
              });

            }
          );
        }
      });

      return result;
    };

  const visibleNodes =
    flattenTree();

  useEffect(() => {

    nodeRefs.current[
      focusedIndex
    ]?.focus();

  }, [focusedIndex]);

  const handleKeyDown =
    event => {

      switch (
        event.key
      ) {

        case "ArrowDown":

          setFocusedIndex(
            prev =>
              Math.min(
                prev + 1,
                visibleNodes.length -
                1
              )
          );

          break;

        case "ArrowUp":

          setFocusedIndex(
            prev =>
              Math.max(
                prev - 1,
                0
              )
          );

          break;

        case "ArrowRight": {

          const node =
            visibleNodes[
              focusedIndex
            ];

          if (
            node.isFolder
          ) {
            setExpanded(
              prev => ({
                ...prev,
                [node.id]:
                  true
              })
            );
          }

          break;
        }

        case "ArrowLeft": {

          const node =
            visibleNodes[
              focusedIndex
            ];

          if (
            node.isFolder
          ) {

            setExpanded(
              prev => ({
                ...prev,
                [node.id]:
                  false
              })
            );
          }

          break;
        }

        case "Enter":

          const node =
            visibleNodes[
              focusedIndex
            ];

          if (
            node.isFolder
          ) {

            setExpanded(
              prev => ({
                ...prev,
                [node.id]:
                  !prev[node.id]
              })
            );
          }

          break;

        default:
          break;
      }
    };

  return (

    <div
      role="tree"
      aria-label="File Explorer"
      onKeyDown={
        handleKeyDown
      }
    >

      {visibleNodes.map(
        (node, index) => {

          const isExpanded =
            expanded[node.id];

          return (

            <div
              key={node.id}

              ref={element => {

                nodeRefs.current[
                  index
                ] = element;
              }}

              role="treeitem"

              tabIndex={
                focusedIndex ===
                index
                  ? 0
                  : -1
              }

              aria-selected={
                focusedIndex ===
                index
              }

              aria-expanded={
                node.isFolder
                  ? isExpanded
                  : undefined
              }

              style={{
                paddingLeft:
                  node.parent
                    ? "30px"
                    : "10px",

                paddingTop:
                  "5px",

                paddingBottom:
                  "5px",

                background:
                  focusedIndex ===
                  index
                    ? "#2563eb"
                    : "white",

                color:
                  focusedIndex ===
                  index
                    ? "white"
                    : "black",

                cursor:
                  "pointer"
              }}
            >

              {node.isFolder
                ? (
                  isExpanded
                    ? "📂 "
                    : "📁 "
                )
                : "📄 "}

              {node.name}

            </div>
          );
        }
      )}

    </div>
  );
}
```

***

# Keyboard Support

| Key           | Action              |
| ------------- | ------------------- |
| ↑ Arrow Up    | Focus previous item |
| ↓ Arrow Down  | Focus next item     |
| → Arrow Right | Expand folder       |
| ← Arrow Left  | Collapse folder     |
| Enter         | Toggle folder       |
| Tab           | Enter/leave tree    |

***

# Focus Management Flow

```text
Documents
     ↓ Arrow Down

Resume.pdf
     ↓ Arrow Down

Notes.docx
     ↓ Arrow Down

Images
```

Focus is controlled via:

```jsx
nodeRefs.current[index]
      ?.focus();
```

***

# Accessibility Best Practices

### ✅ Use Tree Roles

```jsx
role="tree"
role="treeitem"
```

***

### ✅ Use aria-expanded

```jsx
aria-expanded={true}
```

Only for folders.

***

### ✅ Use aria-selected

```jsx
aria-selected={true}
```

For current item.

***

### ✅ Use Roving Tabindex

```jsx
tabIndex={0}
```

Only active element.

```jsx
tabIndex={-1}
```

All others.

***

### ✅ Visual Focus Indicator

```css
:focus {
  outline: 2px solid blue;
}
```

Required for WCAG.

***

# Senior React Interview Answer

A fully accessible file explorer should use:

```text
role="tree"
role="treeitem"
aria-expanded
aria-selected
```

along with keyboard navigation:

```text
Arrow Up
Arrow Down
Arrow Left
Arrow Right
Enter
Space
```

Focus should be managed using an array of `useRef` references and a **roving tabindex** pattern so that only one tree item is tabbable at a time. This approach is commonly used in enterprise React applications that require WCAG-compliant navigation such as file explorers, folder trees, navigation menus, and document management systems.
